posts.title: About Me
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"My professional beginnings were as a Maker, creating digital marketing and enterprise software for big brands. Later, as my interest in applying emerging technologies to human needs grew, my work in user experience led to speaking invitations for local associations and management teams, then to propelling a cross-disciplinary team to the Cisco i-Prize finals.  I soon found myself leading product launch teams, then departments.
 
I am naturally a strategic risk-taker, enthusiastic about new ideas and technologies. But I temper that enthusiasm with the pragmatism of years of experience as a technical architect and a product manager. Having directed a Digital Solutions department ensconced in an IT-focused firm, I appreciate the effort involved in persuading people to think differently: Technology can’t overshadow the needs of either the humans it serves or the businesses it extends. But neither should imaginative advancement be sacrificed to skepticism.

I’ve also persuaded external clients to imagine more: From teaching a custom home builder how to use digital-plus-physical iBeacon technology to create an “at-home” experience for prospective home owners visiting their models, to helping companies shape their customer journey through optimized interactive and email campaigns, and creating digital communities for an urban developer.

I am always eager to guide my teams to reach beyond usability and predictive analytics – as important as they are to understanding the preferences and movements of “users” – and connect with the human beings whose lives are touched by what we do. I’ve always enjoyed the challenge and adventure of leading teams as they move from developing user stories and epics, to iterating between design and development, while driving toward the tight deadlines characteristic of digital product launches. 

![Bill E. Dawson](__GHOST_URL__/content/images/2015/05/BDawson-smile-bw-etching-Twitter-1.png)"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.title: May the Fourth Be With You!
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"May 4th – Star Wars Day! Why is the love of Star Wars synonymous with being a geek in the technology industry? For me Star Wars has always been a glimpse at the possible. A chance to embrace the best of how technology exaggerates the human condition and experience. 

Lose an arm? Bionic replacement. Wanna declare war? Clone an army to fight robots. Need to make the Kessel Run? Jump to Lightspeed!

Sure, a digital calculator and a plastic toy are poor substitutes for the real thing, but it opens the mind to the possibilities! "What if?" is the greatest question in the world for me.

My apologies to the Trekkies, I don't want to start a holy war about between the influences of Star Trek and Star Wars. However, we all know the right answer – Star Wars, [you scruffy nerf herder](http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0489049/)!)

![Fanboys, 2009.](__GHOST_URL__/content/images/2015/05/Unknown.jpeg)"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.title: Happy Mother's Day, TI-99/4a!
posts.html:
A Christmas morning, longer ago than I'd like to admit, our living room TV was blinking "Merry Christmas" in red and green. My mother had programmed a strange device attached to the television with the greeting; it was a Texas Instruments TI-99/4a.

My tech career began with my mother, and I am forever indebted. We spent countless weekends at Radio Shack buying DIY kits and various transistors, capacitors, etc. She patiently sat over my shoulder as I learned to strip wires, solder parts and bring electronics to life. Most importantly, she supported my move into an industry which still grows day-by-day and connects people and devices to create user experiences.

You might not expect a Special Education teacher to be a leading edge programmer, or to support the possibilities of technology, but my mother did. Everyone I have met in this industry owes their career and its success to someone that put them in front of a computer or technology device. Give your thanks this Mother's Day by supporting computer science in school and visit Code.org.

P.S. We miss you Mom, thanks for everything you did for us and all the lives you touched! Janet M. Dawson (1950-2014)

posts.title: Management versus Leadership
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"Too often we use the words *management* and *leadership* interchangeably, and I have watched organizations struggle with the lack of distinction. In my experience _the two are mutually exclusive_, and most folks rarely move well between the definitions.

I have used the following statements from the 1989 book [“On Becoming a Leader” by Warren Bennis](http://www.amazon.com/dp/0465014089/) to illustrate my point in discussions within companies:

> 
> - The manager administers; the leader innovates.
> - The manager is a copy; the leader is an original.
> - The manager maintains; the leader develops.
> - The manager focuses on systems and structure; the leader focuses on people.
> - The manager relies on control; the leader inspires trust.
> - The manager has a short-range view; the leader has a long-range perspective.
> - The manager asks how and when; the leader asks what and why.
> - The manager has his or her eye always on the bottom line; the leader’s eye is on the horizon.
> - The manager imitates; the leader originates.
> - The manager accepts the status quo; the leader challenges it.
> - The manager is the classic good soldier; the leader is his or her own person.
> - The manager does things right; the leader does the right thing.

In which of these definitions do you fall? There is no judgement, both are critical to the success of a team, project or organization!"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Too often we use the words management and leadership interchangeably, and I have watched organizations struggle with the lack of distinction. In my experience the two are mutually exclusive, and most folks rarely move well between the definitions.

I have used the following statements from the 1989 book “On Becoming a Leader” by Warren Bennis to illustrate my point in discussions within companies:

  • The manager administers; the leader innovates.
  • The manager is a copy; the leader is an original.
  • The manager maintains; the leader develops.
  • The manager focuses on systems and structure; the leader focuses on people.
  • The manager relies on control; the leader inspires trust.
  • The manager has a short-range view; the leader has a long-range perspective.
  • The manager asks how and when; the leader asks what and why.
  • The manager has his or her eye always on the bottom line; the leader’s eye is on the horizon.
  • The manager imitates; the leader originates.
  • The manager accepts the status quo; the leader challenges it.
  • The manager is the classic good soldier; the leader is his or her own person.
  • The manager does things right; the leader does the right thing.

In which of these definitions do you fall? There is no judgement, both are critical to the success of a team, project or organization!

posts.title: 3 Rules of Management
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"Almost 20 years ago, my first management position came with some simple words of advice: 

> 1. Give All the Credit
> 1. Take All the Blame
> 1. Never Make a Request You Wouldn't Fulfill

My manager shared and lived these words. His advice has driven my actions on every task, in every position, at every company and team I have served. Although I have gained experience and understanding that influences how I lead, the foundation remains here. Let me explain.

###### 1. Give All the Credit
Your employees and co-workers deserve your appreciation and loyalty. I prefer to lead from encouragement and inspiration. People yearn to achieve and want be recognized. When you ask people to take on work, their success is yours. Make sure to express your gratitude.

###### 2. Take All the Blame
However small the task, when you are asked if it is complete – you rely on someone else to have completed it. As a leader, if your team fails, you have failed. *Own the failure.* Learn from it. Most importantly, help your team grow from it in a constructive way.

###### 3. Never Make a Request You Wouldn't Fulfill
For me, the term 'servant leadership' has become overused – bordering on cliché. *(Yes, I am guilty of using the phrase myself.)* Unfortuantely, it is rarely exercised wth humility and genuine support. Do yourself a favor and invest in your team's success, even if you can't lend a hand. 
> The same boss that imparted these rules pulled an all-nighter with two of us trying to hit a deadline. He couldn't affect the outcome, but he woudln't leave our sides – except to retrieve refreshments and encourage breaks. This act of leadership affects me to this day.

As I mentioned before, I prefer to lead with encouragement and inspiration. These rules have served me well, and I believe have built great teams. What words of wisdom have shaped your leadership style?"}]],"markups":[],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Almost 20 years ago, my first management position came with some simple words of advice:

  1. Give All the Credit
  2. Take All the Blame
  3. Never Make a Request You Wouldn't Fulfill

My manager shared and lived these words. His advice has driven my actions on every task, in every position, at every company and team I have served. Although I have gained experience and understanding that influences how I lead, the foundation remains here. Let me explain.

1. Give All the Credit

Your employees and co-workers deserve your appreciation and loyalty. I prefer to lead from encouragement and inspiration. People yearn to achieve and want be recognized. When you ask people to take on work, their success is yours. Make sure to express your gratitude.

2. Take All the Blame

However small the task, when you are asked if it is complete – you rely on someone else to have completed it. As a leader, if your team fails, you have failed. Own the failure. Learn from it. Most importantly, help your team grow from it in a constructive way.

3. Never Make a Request You Wouldn't Fulfill

For me, the term 'servant leadership' has become overused – bordering on cliché. (Yes, I am guilty of using the phrase myself.) Unfortuantely, it is rarely exercised wth humility and genuine support. Do yourself a favor and invest in your team's success, even if you can't lend a hand.

The same boss that imparted these rules pulled an all-nighter with two of us trying to hit a deadline. He couldn't affect the outcome, but he woudln't leave our sides – except to retrieve refreshments and encourage breaks. This act of leadership affects me to this day.

As I mentioned before, I prefer to lead with encouragement and inspiration. These rules have served me well, and I believe have built great teams. What words of wisdom have shaped your leadership style?

posts.title: The Physics of Santa Claus
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"The following, posted on many websites each year, first appeared in the January 1990 edition of the defunct _Spy Magazine_ under the title “Is there a Santa Claus?” by Richard Waller.

1. No known species of reindeer can fly. BUT there are 300,000 species of living organisms yet to be classified, and while most of these are insects and germs, this does not COMPLETELY rule out flying reindeer, which only Santa has ever seen.

2. There are 2 billion children (persons under 18) in the world. BUT since Santa doesn’t (appear) to handle the Muslim, Hindu, Jewish and Buddhist children, that reduces the workload to 15 percent of the total — 378 million according to Population Reference Bureau. At an average (census) rate of 3.5 children per household, that’s 91.8 million homes. One presumes there’s at least one good child in each.

3. Santa has 31 hours of Christmas to work with, thanks to the different time zones and the rotation of Earth, assuming he travels east to west (which seems logical). This works out to 822.6 visits per second. ... This means Santa’s sleigh is moving at 650 miles per second, 3,000 times the speed of sound. For purposes of comparison, the fastest man-made vehicle on earth, the Ulysses space probe, moves at a poky 27.4 miles per second — a conventional reindeer can run, tops, 15 mph.

4. The payload on the sleigh adds another interesting element. Assuming that each child gets nothing more than a medium-sized Lego set (2 pounds), the sleigh is carrying 321,300 tons, not counting Santa, who is invariably described as overweight. On land, conventional reindeer can pull no more than 300 pounds. Even granting that “flying reindeer” (see point No. 1) could pull TEN TIMES the normal amount, we cannot do the job with eight, or even nine. We need 214,200 reindeer. This increases the payload — not even counting the weight of the sleigh — to 353,430 tons. ... 

5. 353,000 tons traveling at 650 miles per second creates enormous air resistance — this will heat the reindeer up in the same fashion as spacecraft reentering Earth’s atmosphere. The lead pair of reindeer will absorb 14.3 QUINTILLION joules of energy. Per second. Each. In short, they will burst into flame almost instantaneously, exposing the reindeer behind them, and create deafening sonic booms in their wake. The entire reindeer team will be vaporized within 4.26 thousandths of a second. Santa, meanwhile, will be subjected to centrifugal forces 17,500 times greater than gravity. A 250-pound Santa (which seems ludicrously slim) would be pinned to the back of his sleigh by 4,315,015 pounds of force. 

In conclusion — If Santa ever DID deliver presents on Christmas Eve, he’s dead now."}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
The following, posted on many websites each year, first appeared in the January 1990 edition of the defunct Spy Magazine under the title “Is there a Santa Claus?” by Richard Waller.

  1. No known species of reindeer can fly. BUT there are 300,000 species of living organisms yet to be classified, and while most of these are insects and germs, this does not COMPLETELY rule out flying reindeer, which only Santa has ever seen.

  2. There are 2 billion children (persons under 18) in the world. BUT since Santa doesn’t (appear) to handle the Muslim, Hindu, Jewish and Buddhist children, that reduces the workload to 15 percent of the total — 378 million according to Population Reference Bureau. At an average (census) rate of 3.5 children per household, that’s 91.8 million homes. One presumes there’s at least one good child in each.

  3. Santa has 31 hours of Christmas to work with, thanks to the different time zones and the rotation of Earth, assuming he travels east to west (which seems logical). This works out to 822.6 visits per second. ... This means Santa’s sleigh is moving at 650 miles per second, 3,000 times the speed of sound. For purposes of comparison, the fastest man-made vehicle on earth, the Ulysses space probe, moves at a poky 27.4 miles per second — a conventional reindeer can run, tops, 15 mph.

  4. The payload on the sleigh adds another interesting element. Assuming that each child gets nothing more than a medium-sized Lego set (2 pounds), the sleigh is carrying 321,300 tons, not counting Santa, who is invariably described as overweight. On land, conventional reindeer can pull no more than 300 pounds. Even granting that “flying reindeer” (see point No. 1) could pull TEN TIMES the normal amount, we cannot do the job with eight, or even nine. We need 214,200 reindeer. This increases the payload — not even counting the weight of the sleigh — to 353,430 tons. ...

  5. 353,000 tons traveling at 650 miles per second creates enormous air resistance — this will heat the reindeer up in the same fashion as spacecraft reentering Earth’s atmosphere. The lead pair of reindeer will absorb 14.3 QUINTILLION joules of energy. Per second. Each. In short, they will burst into flame almost instantaneously, exposing the reindeer behind them, and create deafening sonic booms in their wake. The entire reindeer team will be vaporized within 4.26 thousandths of a second. Santa, meanwhile, will be subjected to centrifugal forces 17,500 times greater than gravity. A 250-pound Santa (which seems ludicrously slim) would be pinned to the back of his sleigh by 4,315,015 pounds of force.

In conclusion — If Santa ever DID deliver presents on Christmas Eve, he’s dead now.

    gravity. A 250-pound Santa (which seems ludicrously slim) would be pinned to
    the back of his sleigh by 4,315,015 pounds of force.
    
    

In conclusion — If Santa ever DID deliver presents on Christmas Eve, he’s dead
now.

You may have noticed that I use a bow tie for my personal mark and branding. For over a decade it has been an emblem I use to distinguish myself from other speakers/presenters at conferences.

As an outgoing introvert, this symbol also provides me a social crutch. It attracts discussion and conversations, which I might struggle to initiate in a crowded "networking setting."

The interesting thing about any charactersitic is that it can sometimes come to define you. This quirky symbol has become recognizable enough that customers recognize me as "Bowtie Bill." If I wasn't wearing one, they immediately asked me where it was....

Recognizable enough, that on the first day of a new job... they were disappointed I wasn't wearing one. I began codify my brand... every Monday, I participated in a series of meetings with different offices. Therefore I began to wear my bow ties on Monday, as a signifier of "business as usual"... and thus, Bowtie Monday was born.

posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"Multiple articles and editorials have become viral on the potential impact of Conversational UI. The concept is alluring, utopian and evident in our favorite Sci-Fi moments for the last century.

If you've ever been frustrated asking Google or Siri to respond to a request, you can imagine my fear. You can't lose the method of user experience for the sake of something flashy.

To make this work, you need to abstract an experience... and to abstract it, you have to fully understand the interaction – its permutations, pitfalls.

Been stuck on an automated call?"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}
posts.html:
Multiple articles and editorials have become viral on the potential impact of Conversational UI. The concept is alluring, utopian and evident in our favorite Sci-Fi moments for the last century.

If you've ever been frustrated asking Google or Siri to respond to a request, you can imagine my fear. You can't lose the method of user experience for the sake of something flashy.

To make this work, you need to abstract an experience... and to abstract it, you have to fully understand the interaction – its permutations, pitfalls.

Been stuck on an automated call?

posts.title: The CMO-Technologist – My Story
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"*This is the first in a series of blog posts written about the impact of Gartner's 2012 report that CMO spending would outpace CIO spending by 2017.*

My name is Bill E. Dawson, and I am the Chief Information Officer (CIO) of Syndicate Sales, Inc. In over 20 years of consulting in the digital marketing and technology space, I've had to good fortune to work with startups, entrepreneurs and Fortune 100 companies. I may not be your vision of a typical CIO. I'm as comfortable sketching a cartoon, as I am drawing the flow of a web application or discussing system architecture. However, you should know that the definition of CIO is changing and I believe many more will share my career path.


I am a technologist first and foremost. I like to tinker, build and see how things connect. I just happened to have traversed a path that started in user experience, doing user interface design, and coding computer-based training for companies like Novartis, BASF and Maytag. Along this route, I have embraced many roles and fed my curiosities. It is easy to do, if you respectfully ignore boundaries and build collaboration in your organization. 


The question I receive most often is, "Why are you at a *flower* company?" My answer is simple, because they are among the growing list of companies that understand change is inevitable, but you can choose to manage that change in a smart and deliberate way. I think perceptions are stuck in a dogma that marketers are rock stars – flamboyant, budget impervious, distracted artists. In contrast, technologist are stayed, spendthrifts and introverted engineers. The reality is something much more of an amalgam. In this modern age of marketing and technology, one discipline cannot exist without the other.


Every company faces change, and those that successfully navigate it understand the balance of risk and reward.
"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.title: The CMO-Technologist – Gartner Speaks
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","markups":[],"atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"*This is the second in a series of blog posts written about the impact of Gartner's 2012 report that CMO spending would outpace CIO spending by 2017.*

> "By 2017, the CMO will spend more on IT than the CIO."

In 2012 this statement, from Gartner's Laura McLellan, became a rallying cry for the perpetual struggle between Marketing and Techology. Marketers threw up their hands in praise, Technolgoist scowled in disagreement and much discussion began. As we look at 2017 in the rearview mirror, it's time to review these words, reflect on what has transpired and project what might pass.

As a digital marketer, I acknowledge that my platform, it's measures and tactics, are powered by technology. Business owners rely upon measurement and analytics to gauge success of marketing initiatives. How do you prove engagment or ROI on a campaign? You refine every touchpoint and conversions based upon the input of technology. 

As technologists, we can enable the best marketing decisions for our companies. Personas, platforms, channels and traffic can be quantified. As marketers, we can pinpoint the demographic and channel that will provide the best impact and lift for a product or service. When both marketing and technology are working in sync, who benefits? The customer. You can improve the customer experience, through personalization and customization, of every interaction they have with your company.

Why wouldn't you want to marketing and technology to be more collaborative? As customer insight and listening platforms continue to grow, the CIO and CMO will be increasing measured on the same goals and expectations. If a marketing campaign goes viral and creates unexpected traffic and conversions on your site, isn't this a celebration of strategy and tactics – of marketing and technology?

Over the last 5 years since Laura published this prediction, the landscape has shifted. Titles have started to emerge that define the ever blurring lines – Chief Digital Officer, Chief Marketing Technologist, Global Head of Marketing Technology. The question I ask you and your organization, isn't should you adopt one of these titles, rather should you retire the titles of CIO or CMO?


> Bibliography:
> 
> * [Interview with Laura McLellan](http://www.fiercecmo.com/special-reports/gartners-laura-mclellan-digital-marketing-budgets-climb-sales-budgets-are-c)
> * [Presentation on CMT and changing roles, by Laura McLellan, 2014.](http://www.slideshare.net/MarTechConf/chief-marketing-technologists-symbolize-marketings-changing-role-by-laura-mclellan)
> * [_The Rise of the Chief Marketing Technologist_, by Scott Brinker and Laura McLellan.](https://hbr.org/2014/07/the-rise-of-the-chief-marketing-technologist)
"}]],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.title: The CMO-Technologist – Building Your Future
posts.html:
This is the third, and last post, in a series written about the impact of Gartner's 2012 report that CMO spending would outpace CIO spending by 2017.

How can your organization affect change?

The single biggest factor in positive organizational change is your investment in the team. You must have the right talent... As I said in my first post, I like to tinker and build – teams are no different. I am drawn to teammates that embrace ambiguity and change. The moment you are firmly convinced you have all the answers, a new question will arise. The ability to adapt and be flexible is key to your success, and you want good teammates alongside for the ride.

Don't get me wrong, a team of experiementers and chaosticians can wreak havoc. You need the right balance. I have found my best teams have an equilibrium among solders and spies. "Soldiers" are tacticians, and experts in their field. When presented with a task or challenge, they turn to models of experience to resurrect an efficient and appropriate response. "Spies" are strategists, and adapt to meet the goal – often enlisting information and resources outside their expertise.

Once you've gathered a team varied by skill-set and balanced by working types – help them be successful! So, clearly define expectations for your team. They need to understand where their roles will overlap to meet the overarching goal. Conflict could arise if you don't help bridge the gap between marketers and technologists with communication. Remember, the future of marketing-technology is defined by collaboration.

Don't get me wrong, a team of experiementers and chaosticians can wreak havoc. You need the right balance. I have found my best teams have an equilibrium among solders and spies. "Soldiers" are tacticians, and experts in their field. When presented with a task or challenge, they turn to models of experience to resurrect an efficient and appropriate response. "Spies" are strategists, and adapt to meet the goal – often enlisting information and resources outside their expertise.


Once you've gathered a team varied by skill-set and balanced by working types – help them be successful! So, clearly define expectations for your team. They need to understand where their roles will overlap to meet the overarching goal. Conflict could arise if you don't help bridge the gap between marketers and technologists with communication. posts.title: What is privacy?
posts.mobiledoc:
{"version":"0.3.1","atoms":[],"cards":[["markdown",{"cardName":"card-markdown","markdown":"> I was in a restaurant the other day, seated next to a mother with 3 children – ages 7 – 14 years old. Mom had snapped a picture of her girls and was posting it online when a debate broke out with her 10 year old. The daughter was questioning her mother's right to post the picture without her explicit permission.

I found this conversation reassuring! For years, I have believed that younger consumers had no perception of privacy. My eldest niece graduated high school recently and her life story has been published since before she was born. Birth announcement, sonograms, bath time photos, bowel movements, hair cuts – all things we have come to expect (and abhor) published on social media. These missives were posted and de-privatized without her consent. As a matter of fact, she _"inherited"_ a consent already given when, in some cases, she was allowed to take over the social channels branded in her name.

I am not arguing the validity of parental rights, consent or the moral implications. I just want to demonstrate the result. There are generations of consumers that have no concept of "privacy" or "private data." It is an assumed state of openess and digital exchange. The public is finally becoming aware that their data, activities and preferences are currency to the platforms and services they use. Frankly, if you're not paying for a software/service, you should conclude that _you are the product._ 

I would rather ponder how to educate folks that haven't recognized this fact. Are we sufficiently protecting these folks? Or protecting their data in some fashion for our use? What if we found a way to be open and monetize its usage. This would provide value to the user, and room to bargain with what is shared.

"}]],"markups":[],"sections":[[10,0]],"ghostVersion":"3.0"}

posts.html:
I ran cross country in gradeschool. I cannot say it was a good experience, but it was something that has stuck with me. I run to this day...

High school was the first time I trained for running – before that it was organic, being chased, chasing a sibling or soccer ball – never drills and skills. My coach introduced me to rigor and exercise that benefited distance runs. And one bit of advice he always gave was, "charge the hill."

You have to imagine being 4-5 miles into a run and facing a giant hill... not alone, in a group fartlek where the last runner in the line sprints passed you to take the lead. It requires guts, but also consideration for your fellow runners. When you gain the lead, you have to adjust and maintain the pace of the group. Too slow: and you threaten the group with tumbling like dominoes in a collection of clashing shins, calves and tangled limbs. Too fast: and you experience the groans, complaints and threats of a group who will pass you at some point in your run – especially the runner coming from the back of the line to take your place.

Why would someone want to accelerate up a hill? Honestly, it feels like a stupid thing to do, but it is rewarding. Simple math tells you that charging up a hill does not increase your pace/speed. If you're lucky, your extra effort is enough to maintain an overall pace and propel you past competitors.

I am recovering from the first catastrophic injury of my soccer or running career. I obiterated my ACL, tore my MCL and meniscus, and mangled the cartilege in my right knee. Recover has included building strength, but also relearning the coordination that it takes to walk and run at speed. I am charging that hill ever day.

Will you push yourself during difficult times, or hope to keep your head above water? You might surprise yourself – double-down to adversity, and you might just keep pace. Better yet, you may actually find a new speed for the back half of that hill.

it was something that has stuck with me. I run to this day...

High school was the first time I trained for running – before that it was
organic, being chased, chasing a sibling or soccer ball – never drills and
skills. My coach introduced me to rigor and exercise that benefited distance
runs. And one bit of advice he always gave was, "charge the hill."

You have to imagine being 4-5 miles into a run and facing a giant hill... not
alone, in a group fartlek where the last runner in the line sprints passed you
to take the lead. It requires guts, but also consideration for your fellow
runners. When you gain the lead, you have to adjust and maintain the pace of the
group. Too slow: and you threaten the group with tumbling like dominoes in a
collection of clashing shins, calves and tangled limbs. Too fast: and you
experience the groans, complaints and threats of a group who will pass you at
some point in your run – especially the runner coming from the back of the line
to take your place.

Why would someone want to accelerate up a hill? Honestly, it feels like a stupid
thing to do, but it is rewarding. Simple math tells you that charging up a hill
does not increase your pace/speed. If you're lucky, your extra effort is enough
to maintain an overall pace and propel you past competitors. 

I am recovering from the first catastrophic injury of my soccer or running
career. I obiterated my ACL, tore my MCL and meniscus, and mangled the cartilege
in my right knee. Recover has included building strength, but also relearning
the coordination that it takes to walk and run at speed. I am charging that hill
ever day. 

Will you push yourself during difficult times, or hope to keep your head above
water? You might surprise yourself – double-down to adversity, and you might
just keep pace. Better yet, you may actually find a new speed for the back half
of that hill.

posts.title: Building your army
posts.html:
“In this world you’re either growing or you’re dying, so get in motion and grow.” – Lou Holtz

Entrepreneurial businesses strive to grow continuously. Firms pursue new markets, promote new solutions, integrate new teams, etc. Regardless of the objective, your firm has to build a team that can successfully respond to the need for organizational change.

In a previous post, I wrote about the traits I pursue to build successful teams. Since that time I have revised my view and added another resource type. I want to take a second to explain why every team should include soldiers, spies and diplomats.

Soldiers have a singular focus. They are a highly-specialized and proficient in their tasks. You rely on them to achieve a goal with little deviation and high efficiency. They are most successful with clear objectives and flexibility to move indepentently towards that goal. Soldiers will help deliver for your firm and build trust within the organization, but you must provide clear direction and support.

Spies seek context. They gather information and make connections among internal and external teams. You can trust them to gather and collate critical information to help build a clear context of your goals. They develop lines of communications and gather insights that can alert you to potential challenges. Spies will provide you insights on how projects are evolving, so your firm can prepare and adapt to changing dynamics.

Diplomats build relationships. They listen to employees and customers to encourage collaborative action. They monitor progress, provide a buffer to conflicts, motivate teams, and communicate among participants in constructive ways. When difficult converstation are needed, you can trust them to help all parties feel equally represented.

Please don't assume that these resource types inform hierarchy or align to a specific team role! I have found that successful organizational change is more about blending these perspectives in a complementary way than assigning/assuming them to a role or title.

posts.title: Scholarship
posts.html:
A few years ago, an old friend asked for coffee to discuss pursing a post-graduate degree. I had deferred getting an MBA for many years, and she wanted to understand my reasoning. Over thecourse of the conversation, we discussed her motivations, aspirations and hopes once she achieved her degree.

I lost my friend last year, 13 August 2021. The gift she gave me cannot be underestimated.

posts.title: Neural Networks
posts.html:
The rise of machine learning and AI will lead to the first artificial neural network – if it hasn’t emerged already. On November 30, 2022, OpenAI released ChatGPT to the world.

The Internet has attempted that claim for years – due to the connected nature and accessibility of information. democratizing knowledge.

With AI agents and agent swarms – the future is agentic. Imagine creating AI that automates specialization for your firm. Tax accountant, business lawyer, brand voice, venture mathmatics, etc. Than you release these perspectives in a manner that provides chaining and collaborative logic... like a well-functioning team, or a human brain.

posts.title: AI ‘s Specialization Era
posts.html:
We’ve seen this story before. It just took us a while to recognize the pattern.

Early automobiles were massive, complex machines that only the wealthy could afford. They required specialized knowledge to operate and maintain. Over decades, they became smaller, more efficient, and purpose-built—sedans for families, trucks for hauling, sports cars for performance. The technology didn’t disappear, it just specialized.

AI is following the same arc, and most people are still stuck watching the dinosaurs.

The Monolith Phase

Right now, we’re enamored with massive language models. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — these are the equivalent of those early automobiles: impressive, powerful, and designed to do everything for everyone. We’re in the proof-of-concept phase, where scale equals capability and generalization is the goal.

But this phase doesn’t last. It never does.

The current model—send your data to a massive cloud-based system, get a response, repeat—works for early adoption. It doesn’t work for ubiquity. It’s too expensive, too slow, and too exposed.

The Specialization Shift

What comes next isn’t bigger models. It’s smaller, focused ones that live where you need them.

A healthcare model on your smartwatch that knows your baseline vitals, recognizes anomalies, and coaches you through interventions—without ever transmitting your heart rate data to a server farm in Virginia.

A scheduling assistant embedded in your calendar that understands your work patterns, energy levels, and priorities—making decisions locally based on your behavior, not a generalized dataset.

A nutrition model in your refrigerator that tracks what you actually eat, suggests recipes based on what’s expiring, and adjusts recommendations as your habits change—all without reporting your midnight ice cream runs to anyone.

These aren’t hypothetical. They’re inevitable.

Why Specialization Wins

Economics: Running a massive general-purpose model every time someone asks about dinner is absurd. A focused model trained on nutrition and cooking is faster, cheaper, and better at the specific task.

Privacy: When the model lives on your device and the data never leaves, the entire surveillance economy around AI collapses. You can’t monetize data you never collect.

Performance: A specialized model optimized for one domain will always outperform a generalist trying to be adequate at everything.

Accessibility: Smaller models mean lower computational requirements, which means AI capabilities spread to devices that can’t afford constant cloud connectivity.

The Privacy Paradox Solved

The current AI gold rush has a dirty secret: it requires your data. Every query you make, every interaction you have, feeds the system. The business model depends on centralization.

Specialized, local models break this dependency. When your health AI lives on your wrist and your scheduling AI lives in your phone, the data stays with you. The model improves based on your usage, not everyone’s.

This isn’t altruism—it’s necessity. Regulatory pressure, consumer awareness, and basic economics will force this shift. The companies that figure out how to monetize specialized local AI will define the next decade. The ones clinging to massive centralized models will become cautionary tales.

What This Means for Builders

If you’re building in the AI space, the question isn’t whether to specialize—it’s where. The general-purpose wars are over before most companies realize the fight has moved.

The opportunity is in verticalization. Pick a problem domain. Build a model that solves it exceptionally well. Make it small enough to run locally. Give users control of their data.

We’re moving from the age of AI dinosaurs to the age of AI mammals—smaller, more efficient, better adapted to their specific environments.

The giants will still exist. But they won’t be where the innovation happens.

The technology didn’t disappear, it just ","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":2,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"specialized","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":".","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"AI is following the same arc, and most people are still stuck watching the dinosaurs.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The Monolith Phase","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"extended-heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Right now, we’re enamored with massive language models. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — these are the equivalent of those early automobiles: impressive, powerful, and designed to do everything for everyone. We’re in the proof-of-concept phase, where scale equals capability and generalization is the goal.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":2,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"But this phase doesn’t last. It never does.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The current model—send your data to a massive cloud-based system, get a response, repeat—works for early adoption. It doesn’t work for ubiquity. It’s too expensive, too slow, and too exposed.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The Specialization Shift","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"extended-heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"What comes next isn’t bigger models. It’s smaller, focused ones that live where you need them.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"A healthcare model on your smartwatch that knows your baseline vitals, recognizes anomalies, and coaches you through interventions—without ever transmitting your heart rate data to a server farm in Virginia.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"A scheduling assistant embedded in your calendar that understands your work patterns, energy levels, and priorities—making decisions locally based on your behavior, not a generalized dataset.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"A nutrition model in your refrigerator that tracks what you actually eat, suggests recipes based on what’s expiring, and adjusts recommendations as your habits change—all without reporting your midnight ice cream runs to anyone.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"These aren’t hypothetical. They’re inevitable.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Why Specialization Wins","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"extended-heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":1,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Economics","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":": Running a massive general-purpose model every time someone asks about dinner is absurd. A focused model trained on nutrition and cooking is faster, cheaper, and better at the specific task.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":1,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Privacy","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":": When the model lives on your device and the data never leaves, the entire surveillance economy around AI collapses. You can’t monetize data you never collect.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":1,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Performance","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":": A specialized model optimized for one domain will always outperform a generalist trying to be adequate at everything.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":1,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Accessibility","type":"extended-text","version":1},{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":": Smaller models mean lower computational requirements, which means AI capabilities spread to devices that can’t afford constant cloud connectivity.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The Privacy Paradox Solved","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"extended-heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The current AI gold rush has a dirty secret: it requires your data. Every query you make, every interaction you have, feeds the system. The business model depends on centralization.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"Specialized, local models break this dependency. When your health AI lives on your wrist and your scheduling AI lives in your phone, the data stays with you. The model improves based on your usage, not everyone’s.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"This isn’t altruism—it’s necessity. Regulatory pressure, consumer awareness, and basic economics will force this shift. The companies that figure out how to monetize specialized local AI will define the next decade. The ones clinging to massive centralized models will become cautionary tales.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"What This Means for Builders","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"extended-heading","version":1,"tag":"h2"},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"If you’re building in the AI space, the question isn’t whether to specialize—it’s where. The general-purpose wars are over before most companies realize the fight has moved.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The opportunity is in verticalization. Pick a problem domain. Build a model that solves it exceptionally well. Make it small enough to run locally. Give users control of their data.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"We’re moving from the age of AI dinosaurs to the age of AI mammals—smaller, more efficient, better adapted to their specific environments.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1},{"children":[{"detail":0,"format":0,"mode":"normal","style":"","text":"The giants will still exist. But they won’t be where the innovation happens.","type":"extended-text","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"paragraph","version":1}],"direction":"ltr","format":"","indent":0,"type":"root","version":1}}
